schools.summary: Augustine: rejoice rather than wonder — it is greater to create than to raise; Bede: Lazarus (‘helped’) was most helped, four days dead.
Bethany ~2 miles east of Jerusalem (now El Azariyeh); John alone records it, probably because Lazarus still lived and could be persecuted (12:10); link to the parable (Lk 16:20).
Calvin: the raising is ‘a lively image of our future resurrection,’ the concluding act of his ministry, his power shown on a rotting corpse.
Chrysostom: those dearest to God endure such trials — Lazarus, Christ’s friend, fell sick.

A parenthesis identifying Mary by the well-known anointing; most read it as a proleptic allusion to 12:3 (the aorist timeless), though Lightfoot takes it of a past event; not the ‘sinner’ of Lk 7 nor Mary Magdalene.
Calvin: it is ignorance to confuse this Mary with the sinner of Lk 7 — Christ was anointed on different occasions; the past tense refers to the time of writing.

debates.question: Does ‘anointed’ refer to the (future) anointing of John 12 told proleptically, or an earlier one?

sides.position: a past anointing already known to the reader

schools.summary: The sisters ‘send’ a message of touching simplicity — not dictating a cure but stating the need with the forcible plea ‘he whom thou lovest is sick’; a model of believing prayer and lawful Christian friendship.
Chrysostom: like the centurion they ‘sent, not went,’ from faith and sorrow, surprised that one Christ loved could fall sick.
‘Not unto death’ = death is not the final issue but the occasion of a greater miracle; ‘that’ marks the event, not the cause; the double end is the Father’s and the Son’s glory, inseparable.
Augustine: ‘that the Son of God might be glorified’ has Christ calling himself God by implication — confounding those who deny the Son is God.
Calvin: the afflictions of God’s people are ordered not to destruction but to the manifesting of God’s glory and their good.
A real, particular love (agapaō) for each of the three, who were among Christ’s intimate friends (Lk 10:38); Martha named first as the elder of the household.
Calvin: the love is recorded just here so we do not stumble at the coming delay — Christ’s love is not measured by haste and may withhold help for a greater good.

The two-day delay let Lazarus truly die and lie four days (v.39), so the death was beyond doubt; it shows he meant to raise him; Ellicott grounds it in his Father-marked ‘hours’ (2:4), not in testing faith.
Chrysostom/Alcuin: he waited for death and burial — ‘he stinks’ — so none could call it a trance; the miracle thereby more wonderful.
Calvin: Christ’s love does not always answer at once and may withhold help for a greater end — the delay is design, not coldness.
After the two days he resolves ‘let us go into Judea again’ — ‘again’ recalling the recent escape from stoning (10:39-40); a deliberate return toward danger for Lazarus’ sake.
Calvin: the summons answers the fear that Christ had forgotten Lazarus; he shows he cared. Henry: Christ goes with his people into danger, and their day lasts till their work is done.
The recent stoning attempt (10:31, weeks before) made return seem ‘suicidal madness’; the disciples fear for Christ and for themselves (cf. 11:16).
Calvin: they dissuade him from fear for themselves, dressing cowardice as concern for their Master — as men daily disguise dread of the cross as prudence.

The day’s twelve hours figure the appointed span of his life; walking by daylight (the Father’s purpose) one ‘stumbleth not’ (cf. 9:4) — not fatalism but the opposite of cowardice; his enemies cannot shorten his day.
Calvin: the plain sense — safe travel by sunlight; Wesley: a determined time God has allotted, within which no snare can prevail.
From natural night (Eccl 2:14) the verse passes to spiritual darkness: ‘no light in him’ shifts the lamp within — man has the capacity for light but is not its source (Mt 6:23; 8:12).
Wesley: to ‘walk in the night’ is to lack God’s light and protecting providence.
‘Sleepeth’ (koimaomai) is the NT euphemism for a believer’s death, so gentle the disciples mistake it for rest; for the believer death is but a sleep from which Christ will wake him.
Augustine: sleeping to the Lord, dead only to men who cannot raise him; he awoke him as easily as one wakes a sleeper; ‘that ye may believe’ = believe more deeply.
Chrysostom: ‘our friend’ shows how bound they were to go; the errand is to awaken, not to dispute with the Jews.
Sleep was a Jewish (and general) sign of recovery (Lightfoot; Menander’s proverb), so they reason ‘if he be fallen asleep he shall be saved’ — no need to risk Judea.
Calvin: not a crafty pretence but a genuine misunderstanding they gladly seize as an excuse to avoid the danger.

themes: ‘if he sleep, he shall do well’

schools.summary: John’s aside confirms the disciples genuinely misunderstood — taking ‘sleep’ (koimēsis, only here in NT) literally, the surface sense that naturally suggested itself, though three had heard the metaphor before (Mt 9:24).
Seeing the figure missed, Christ speaks ‘plainly’ (parrēsia, without metaphor) — ‘Lazarus died’ — words that fall short of the deeper truth but are the only ones they can grasp.
Calvin: astonishing patience with their gross ignorance; he delayed the Spirit’s fuller grace so that their later renewal in a moment might be the greater.

His gladness is not at the death but that he was absent — present, he would have been entreated to heal; raising after four days is the greater, more faith-confirming wonder (‘that ye may come to believe’).
Calvin: his absence served their faith, for works nearer the course of nature are less valued; the resurrection had to be delayed to be known as truly divine.

themes: faith confirmed by the greater miracle

schools.summary: Thomas/Didymus (‘twin’) rallies the disciples to ‘die with him’ — with Lazarus (Barnes, ironically) or with Jesus (Wesley); a loyalty ready to follow Christ to death.
Calvin: he follows without confidence — ‘the language of despair,’ expecting defeat rather than resting on Christ’s promise; brave love without faith (cf. 20:24).

debates.question: ‘That we may die with him’ — with whom?

sides.position: with Jesus, whom the Jews will kill

schools.summary: The chronology (messenger + two days’ wait + journey) yields four days; Lazarus was unembalmed (v.39) so the corruption was real and the death undoubted; the journey itself included good works (Jericho, Zacchaeus).
Augustine/Alcuin read the four days as four deaths/stages of sin (or the deepening of sin from elation to habit), all of which Christ can awaken; the delay made the miracle more glorious.

outliers.note: Allegorises the four days as four kinds of spiritual death Christ is able to raise.

schools.summary: ‘Fifteen furlongs’ = about two miles; the nearness to Jerusalem meant many Jews could come and the news reach the Sanhedrin; the past tense ‘was’ hints John wrote after the city’s fall.
Calvin: the detail serves the narrative’s certainty — by a secret decree of God the Jerusalem mourners are gathered as witnesses, so the miracle could not remain unknown.
Jewish mourning (seven days of lamentation within thirty) brought many Jerusalem friends — a sign of the family’s prominence; the comforters were probably not hostile.
Calvin: their aim was consolation, but God’s aim was to gather witnesses; grief itself is no sin unless corrupted by excess.

The sisters’ characters show even in grief: active Martha (the household manager, Lk 10:40) hurries out; Mary sits in the posture of mourning (Job 2:8). Martha goes out to speak apart and to spare Christ the watching crowd.
Calvin: Martha goes beyond the village to meet him more secretly, his danger being fresh; Wesley: Mary, sitting within, likely did not hear.

‘If thou hadst been here’ (a saying the sisters had repeated, cf. v.3, 32) mingles real faith with wistful longing and a hint of reproach; their faith was incomplete — power thought near at hand, not at a distance.
Calvin: a modest complaint, partly from faith but mixed with disorderly passions — she gives way to feeling instead of restraining it under faith’s rule.
A half-formed hope she dare not utter — that even now God would grant Christ her brother’s life (recalling Jairus’ daughter, the widow’s son); she speaks indirectly, intimating without asking.
‘Thy brother shall rise again’ (anastēsetai) is designedly ambiguous — Martha takes it of the last-day resurrection; Christ meets her on the ground she confesses before lifting her higher.
Calvin: a promise she can grasp, framed to draw out her faith — her retreat to ‘the last day’ (v.24) gives occasion for the great self-disclosure.
Martha falls back on the orthodox Pharisaic hope of a general resurrection ‘at the last day’ (Dan 12:2; Jn 5:28) — real faith, but pushed off to the far future, missing the nearer hope.
Calvin: she ‘restricts’ grace to the last day, her faith creeping on the ground — sound in doctrine but too small a grasp of Christ, whom he will now enlarge.

‘I am the resurrection and the life’ shifts hope from a future doctrine to Christ’s present person — he is the author and embodiment of resurrection (1 Cor 1:30), ‘I and none beside me,’ present not merely at the last day.
Calvin: resurrection comes first (the race is plunged in death) then life (he preserves it to the end); both the spiritual raising from sin and the bodily pledge are lodged in Christ.
Origen frames the move from the ‘sensible’ to the ‘spiritual’ Gospel — the bodily sign pointing to the spiritual resurrection.

themes: ‘I am the resurrection and the life’

schools.summary: The living believer ‘shall never die’ — physical death not denied but made the passage to higher life; the death excluded is eternal/spiritual death (8:51; 6:50).
Calvin: the deathlessness rests on Christ’s power to preserve the life he gives (1 Pet 1:23); ‘Believest thou this?’ presses the doctrine home as a personal demand for trust.

A noble confession (the perfect ‘I have believed’ = settled faith) that, made with her brother four days dead, ‘outranks’ Peter’s (Mt 16:16; cf. 6:69) — though she runs off before grasping every depth.
Calvin: confessing him ‘the Christ, the Son of God’ includes the sum of all blessings — strengthened by the prophets, she may expect from him the full restoration of all things.
Martha calls Mary ‘secretly’ to avoid the hostile Jews (v.46); ‘the Master calleth for thee’ shows Christ asked for Mary by name (to strengthen her faith); ‘Master’ = the family’s name for him.
Chrysostom: Christ’s words stopped Martha’s grief; Augustine: she spoke low; Bede: Mary, overwhelmed, will say less for weeping.
Mary ‘arose quickly’ (ēgerthē tachy) — the summons of Christ lifts her at once from the sitting posture of grief (v.20); her swiftness measures the love of one who had sat at his feet (Lk 10:39).

themes: raised from grief to seek Christ

schools.summary: Jesus stayed outside the village near the (extramural) burying-place; he tarried where he met Martha, perhaps (Westcott) to meet the sisters away from the crowd of mourners.
Calvin: he stays by another design than Martha’s — that the Jews might be gathered as witnesses of the miracle.
The comforters, supposing Mary went to weep at the grave (a mourning custom), follow her — kindly meant but defeating Martha’s secrecy (v.28); kind notions often do the wrong thing.
Calvin: the frustrated secrecy is God’s secret providence — the mourners are led, unintending, to become witnesses of the miracle.
Mary falls impulsively at his feet and speaks Martha’s very words (v.21) but no more, only weeping — her emotion too deep for speech; she ‘makes amends’ for her slowness (Wesley).
Calvin: her prostration shows she owned him as the Son of God, beyond ordinary honour — yet she shares Martha’s fault of limiting his power to his bodily presence.

‘Groaned’ (enebrimēsato, ‘to snort’) = vehement agitation; Barnes reads grief not anger, while the Jews’ ‘perfunctory’ wailing may add indignation; Christ is shown truly man, weeping with those who weep.
Augustine: ‘who but Himself could trouble Him?’ — he was troubled because it pleased him; his affections are free, the Word having fitted soul and flesh to himself.
Calvin: real sympathy weeping with the mourners, yet contemplating something higher — the general misery of the human race under death.
Wesley: ‘he troubled himself’ — his affections were not passions but voluntary emotions wholly in his own power.

sides.position: grief and sympathy (moved by the mourners’ sorrow)
indignation (at the perfunctory wailing, or at death/unbelief)

themes: sympathy and the misery of death

schools.summary: The omniscient Lord asks ‘as man’ (cf. 6:6) — to avoid any suspicion of collusion, raise expectation, and draw the Jews to the tomb as witnesses; ‘come and see’ echoes 1:46.
Augustine: he asked to try their faith and make them seek the miracle, with an allusion to our hidden calling; Cyril: some are saved by others’ faith (the sisters’ for Lazarus).

The shortest verse proves Christ truly man and a tender friend; ‘wept’ (edakrysen, silent tears, unlike the wailing klaiō) — grief over Lazarus, the sisters, and the unbelieving Jews; tears at the grave are no sin (Rom 12:15).
The first party reads his tears as genuine love (ephilei, ‘used to love,’ as in v.3) — ‘how he must have loved him’; Clarke applies it to the cross: ‘behold how he hath loved us.’
Calvin: of the two opinions, these speak more candidly though they ascribe to him only what is human, unlike the cavillers of v.37.

themes: the love shown at the cross

schools.summary: A second, hostile party turns his tears into a cavil, appealing to the blind-man miracle (ch.9) to insinuate he could not (ouk edynato) have prevented the death — a strange mixture of half-faith and unbelief.
Cyril cites the prophecy of opened eyes and raised dead (Isa 35:5), pointing past their doubt to the sign Christ will give.
The renewed groaning (embrimōmenos, as v.33) is stirred by the cavil of v.37 — holy indignation at their malice; the tomb is a rock cave closed with a great stone (cf. Machpelah, Gen 49).
Calvin: he comes ‘as a champion preparing for a contest,’ groaning at the tyranny of death before his eyes — contemplating the thing itself rather than the men.

sides.position: agitation at death itself, which he comes to conquer

schools.summary: The stone is removed so the corpse may be seen and smelt — no fallacy; the four days and corruption (‘he is a fourth-day man,’ stinketh) prove a real, undeniable death, ‘marks of truth.’
Calvin: Martha’s ‘he stinketh’ is distrust — measuring God’s infinite power by the flesh, inferring no remedy from putrefaction; reason and faith struggle (Wesley).
Christ recalls his promise (substance of vv.4, 23-26) and reproves Martha’s decline from her earlier faith (v.22); faith alone ‘interests the saving power of God.’
Calvin: faith both opens the eyes to see God’s power and ‘prepares the way’ for it — first believing, then seeing the glory of God.
The lifted eyes and thanksgiving are spoken before the unbelieving crowd, that they may know the work is by God’s power (not the devil’s); Christ has the answer before he acts.
Gregory/Augustine: the three raisings figure three depths of sin (heart, act, habit), all of which grace reaches; Alcuin: as man, inferior, he prays.
Chrysostom: the lowly words aim not at sublimity but at drawing us; Origen: lift the mind in prayer after Christ’s pattern.
Calvin: a mind truly prepared for prayer, yet the thanks show he is heard always; Wesley: he acts with absolute sovereignty, thanking only for the opportunity.

debates.question: Does the prayer mark dependence or sovereignty?

sides.position: true prayer of the Son, dependent as man (Alcuin) yet always heard
an act of absolute sovereignty, not a petition for aid

schools.summary: The audible prayer was for the bystanders, not for himself — ‘I knew that thou hearest me always’ (a settled certainty rooted in the union of wills, 10:30), that they may believe he was sent.
Calvin: an anticipation lest any doubt his favour with the Father — he needed not pray, executing only the Father’s will, yet called on the Name to assure men it is a divine work.

The ‘loud voice’ (unlike magicians’ muttering, Isa 8:19) is for the crowd, that they see Lazarus come forth simultaneously with the command — fulfilling 5:25; he names him as one waking a sleeping friend (cf. 20:16).
Calvin: by word, not touch, his divine power and the efficacy of his word are shown — the same voice that gives spiritual life daily and will raise all the dead.

Lazarus comes forth still wound in graveclothes (keiriai) and napkin (soudarion) — Lightfoot’s ‘second miracle’ of walking out so bound; ‘Loose him’ gives the bystanders what they can do, making them witnesses of a real raising.

Calvin and others: a picture of the gospel — Christ raises by his word, then would have the freed soul loosed from the grave-clothes of its old bondage to walk; a pledge of the resurrection of all his own.
Many eye-witnesses (not hearsay) believe — the miracle an incontestable proof; their visit of consolation became their conversion (Eccl 7:2); the usual double effect of Christ’s works.
Calvin: miracles prepare for or confirm faith — here they prepare, drawing reverence, though the bare sign needs the word and Spirit to produce faith; Wesley: the Son thereby glorified (v.4).
The same clear miracle hardens some, who report to the Pharisees — whether as informers (Gill, Clarke) or, more charitably, as convinced witnesses demanding a verdict (Ellicott, Robertson).
Calvin: detestable ingratitude — hearts must be purified to profit by miracles; Wesley: proof that those who hear not Moses are unmoved though one rise from the dead (Lk 16:31).

debates.question: In what spirit did these go to the Pharisees?

sides.position: as hostile informers, hardened against the miracle
as convinced witnesses reporting it as proof / demanding a judgment

schools.summary: The Sanhedrin (chief-priest Sadducees and Pharisees combined) meets; their own question ‘this man doeth many miracles’ concedes the fact they should have believed, yet they plot instead.
Theophylact/Chrysostom: a miracle that should draw praise becomes a reason to plot; with his divinity proved, they call him ‘only a man’; Augustine: they consult how to kill, not how to be saved.
Calvin/Henry: monstrous blindness — they assemble to bury the glory of God, a clear discovery of the heart’s enmity against God.
Political fear: ‘all’ will believe and Rome will destroy ‘place and nation’ (temple and city, held at Rome’s will); Lightfoot: they half-knew the truth (cf. the heir, Mk 12:7).
Calvin: under pretext of the public good they set themselves against God; the very ruin they schemed to prevent they brought on themselves by killing the Messiah.
‘High priest that same year’ marks the office made near-annual under Rome and the fateful year; Caiaphas cuts through the council with a hardened politician’s scorn, proposing one man’s death.
Chrysostom: the Spirit drew a prophecy from a wicked man — the office, not his corrupt heart, made him prophesy unconsciously.
Origen: not all who prophesy are prophets — Caiaphas prophesied like Balaam, meaning only to incite his death.
On the surface a cold raison d’état — sacrifice one man to spare the nation from Rome; ‘expedient’ and ‘die for the people’ (hyper) carry, unintended, the gospel sense of the one dying for all.

themes: ‘one man should die for the people’

schools.summary: Caiaphas meant only his cruel design, yet God overruled his tongue to utter, ‘not of himself,’ a true (unconscious) prophecy of the atonement — like Balaam, ‘the oracle of God in spite of himself.’
Calvin: a higher impulse guided his tongue — he had ‘two tongues,’ one voicing his murderous design, the other proclaiming the death’s saving purpose.
John widens the prophecy: not for the nation only but to gather the scattered ‘children of God’ — the ‘other sheep’ (10:16) into one flock, Jew and Gentile (1 John 2:2; Eph 2:14).
Calvin: reconciliation extends to the Gentiles; the scattered are called ‘children of God’ by election, before their calling, though yet wandering enemies.
On that day the Sanhedrin officially decreed Christ’s death (only the manner remaining, to avoid tumult, Mt 26:4); the raising of Lazarus ‘brought matters to a head,’ a month before the end.
Calvin: they had already resolved on his death; only the method remained — the sign of life becomes the trigger of the death-warrant.
He withdraws to Ephraim (hill country near Bethel) — prudence, not fear; he never exposed his life needlessly, and would not force the end before his hour (the Passover, a month off).
Augustine: not that his power failed, but to teach by example that believers may lawfully retire from danger; Chrysostom: the troubled disciples ‘continued with him’ (Lk 22:28).
Calvin: he fled not to escape the Father’s calling but to present himself for voluntary death at the appointed time; Henry: no devices of man alter God’s purposes.
The last Passover of the ministry draws near; pilgrims go up early to purify themselves by the rabbinic rule (cf. Num 9:10) — the feast at which the true Passover Lamb will be slain.
Henry: as they purified before the feast, so before our gospel passover we must renew repentance and solemnly prepare to meet God.
The festival crowds, knowing both his fame and the rulers’ hostility, seek and debate in the temple whether he will dare come to the feast (expecting ‘no,’ given the decree of v.57).
Calvin and others: devotion and danger mingle — the purified pilgrims speculate about the condemned man; the hush before the last great act.
The Sanhedrin issues an open order that any who know his whereabouts must inform, to seize him — explaining his withdrawal to Ephraim (v.54) and setting the stage for the betrayal.

themes: taken only at his chosen hour
